title: Politics Why Minnesotans Still Haven't Backed Down From Fighting ICE It's freezing
raw_response: {"rating": -7.0, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a long-form narrative/opinion piece presented as immersive journalism. The author's own prose consistently frames ICE agents as villains and community resisters as heroic, adopting the perspective of anti-ICE activists throughout. The author even breaks journalistic neutrality by explicitly joining the protest (taking a whistle), and uses highly charged language in their own voice to describe ICE operations. The sustained, one-directional framing with no counterbalancing perspective warrants a strong Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author refers to ICE agents as 'bastards' in their own narrative voice ('Whatever the bastards had planned for the apartment complex will have to wait for another day'), an explicitly derogatory characterization. (2) The author describes ICE as a threat to be warned about, using language like 'secret police coming to abduct them, disappear them,' framing federal immigration enforcement as authoritarian oppression in their own words. (3) The author explicitly breaks journalistic neutrality by accepting a protest whistle and participating in the demonstration, narrating this decision sympathetically ('Hell with it. I take the whistle'). (4) The headline and subhead frame the piece entirely from the activist perspective: 'Fighting ICE,' 'It's terrifying. And it's potentially deadly. But anti-ICE activists are not giving up.' (5) The author frames ICE's presence with the language 'ICE is here, and ICE has killed, and ICE may kill again' — a dramatic, accusatory construction in their own voice with no context about enforcement rationale, legal authority, or the circumstances of any deaths. (6) The author uses a predatory metaphor for themselves as a journalist ('Just as roadkill summons vultures, so too does human misery summon journalists'), framing the situation as unambiguous human suffering inflicted by ICE. (7) No government perspective, ICE spokesperson, legal justification, or any counterbalancing viewpoint is included anywhere in the piece. The enforcement side is presented exclusively through menacing imagery (guns, flak vests, aggressive driving, zoom lenses). (8) The author draws a direct parallel between ICE enforcement and the George Floyd protests, linking immigration enforcement to police violence against Black Americans, a distinctly left-aligned framing. (9) Embedded article links ('The Frightening, Very Real Tool ICE Agents Have,' 'Trump's Plan for an American Gulag') reinforce a left-aligned editorial ecosystem."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a long-form narrative/opinion piece presented as immersive journalism. The author's own prose consistently frames ICE agents as villains and community resisters as heroic, adopting the perspective of anti-ICE activists throughout. The author even breaks journalistic neutrality by explicitly joining the protest (taking a whistle), and uses highly charged language in their own voice to describe ICE operations. The sustained, one-directional framing with no counterbalancing perspective warrants a strong Left rating. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author refers to ICE agents as 'bastards' in their own narrative voice ('Whatever the bastards had planned for the apartment complex will have to wait for another day'), an explicitly derogatory characterization. (2) The author describes ICE as a threat to be warned about, using language like 'secret police coming to abduct them, disappear them,' framing federal immigration enforcement as authoritarian oppression in their own words. (3) The author explicitly breaks journalistic neutrality by accepting a protest whistle and participating in the demonstration, narrating this decision sympathetically ('Hell with it. I take the whistle'). (4) The headline and subhead frame the piece entirely from the activist perspective: 'Fighting ICE,' 'It's terrifying. And it's potentially deadly. But anti-ICE activists are not giving up.' (5) The author frames ICE's presence with the language 'ICE is here, and ICE has killed, and ICE may kill again' — a dramatic, accusatory construction in their own voice with no context about enforcement rationale, legal authority, or the circumstances of any deaths. (6) The author uses a predatory metaphor for themselves as a journalist ('Just as roadkill summons vultures, so too does human misery summon journalists'), framing the situation as unambiguous human suffering inflicted by ICE. (7) No government perspective, ICE spokesperson, legal justification, or any counterbalancing viewpoint is included anywhere in the piece. The enforcement side is presented exclusively through menacing imagery (guns, flak vests, aggressive driving, zoom lenses). (8) The author draws a direct parallel between ICE enforcement and the George Floyd protests, linking immigration enforcement to police violence against Black Americans, a distinctly left-aligned framing. (9) Embedded article links ('The Frightening, Very Real Tool ICE Agents Have,' 'Trump's Plan for an American Gulag') reinforce a left-aligned editorial ecosystem.
